figures.caption: Figure 1: Qualitative comparison on the prompt “No one is in the room but there are chairs. high quality, 8k.” I.I.D (top): Low diversity between samples. CADS (upper-middle): High-quality samples but with low prompt adherence (people in the second sample). PG (lower-middle): Increase in variety with visible artifacts. EDDY (bottom): Diverse output that preserves photorealism and prompt fidelity.
Table 11: PG chosen hyperparameters per diversity level. Stop ratio sr = 0.2 is fixed across all settings.
Table 10: EDDY chosen hyperparameters per diversity level. Stop ratio sr = 0.2 and Hutchinson trace samples m = 25 are fixed across all settings.

Table 1: Qualitative results for the synthetic experiment. The trade-off is controlled in EDDY by the bandwidth wg, and in PG by the guidance weight wg. We measure both test statistics and p-value. Under the null-hypothesis both distributions are identical.

Figure 5: Diversity–quality trade-off curves for EDDY and Particle Guidance (PG) on MS-COCO. Each point corresponds to a different guidance strength; the white star marks the I.I.D baseline. The x-axis measures pairwise DINO similarity (← lower = more diverse). EDDY consistently Pareto-dominates PG across all quality metrics (CLIPScore, Aesthetic, CMMD, FID) at matched diversity levels.

Table 2: Pareto frontiers in (a) FLUX.1-dev and (b). DINO Sim represent similarity between pairwise samples (lower = more diverse). FID and CMMD represent quality and computed as the mean of variation ℓ of compared algorithm vs. variation (ℓ+1) mod n of I.I.D (lower = higher quality). CLIPScore represent prompt alignment (higher = more aligned).
